does: This paper addresses hallucination in large vision-language models by framing it as a preference learning problem. The core contribution is Hallucination-Aware Direct Preference Optimization (HA-DPO), which trains models to prefer non-hallucinatory responses over hallucinatory ones when both are presented for the same image. The authors develop an efficient pipeline to construct paired datasets of accurate and fabricated descriptions that maintain stylistic consistency, enabling robust preference learning. When applied to three mainstream multimodal models including MiniGPT-4, HA-DPO substantially reduces hallucination rates and improves generalization performance across diverse visual reasoning tasks, demonstrating measurable improvements in both accuracy and consistency.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in preference-based optimization for language models, extending direct preference optimization methods from NLP to the multimodal domain where hallucination remains a critical unresolved problem. The paper responds to growing recognition that standard supervised fine-tuning fails to adequately suppress false content generation in vision-language models, even as instruction-tuning has improved general capabilities. It connects to prior work on hallucination detection and mitigation in VLMs while adopting techniques from RLHF and DPO literature that have proven effective for aligning model behaviors in text-only settings. The approach implicitly builds on insights about preference learning as an alternative to reinforcement learning for behavioral alignment.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more reliable multimodal AI systems by showing preference optimization can effectively reduce hallucinations without additional computational overhead of reinforcement learning. The efficient dataset construction pipeline suggests scalable methods for creating contrastive training examples from existing data. Future work could extend HA-DPO to other vision-language tasks beyond visual question answering, combine it with other mitigation strategies like chain-of-thought prompting, or apply similar preference-based frameworks to other failure modes in multimodal models. The approach may also motivate investigation into whether preference learning can address related problems in grounded language generation and embodied AI where factual accuracy matters critically.
